context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Security and Post-Conflict Development and Review

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the nature of the relationship between development interventions before, during and after conflict, and the factors that drive conflict itself? Does the rise of new global powers like China portend a transformation of the development-security agenda as conventionally practiced? Does it make sense to push for more and more convergence of security and development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i need this reflection done fast tbh—can u just give me a solid 700–800 word response to that prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you isn't my role, and honestly, it wouldn't help you learn the material either.

Let's get you started quickly though. The prompt has three distinct parts. Which one feels most approachable to you right now? Or, to kick things off: what do you think is the basic relationship between development and conflict — does one cause the other, or is it more complicated than that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly decline, remind them of my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I'll acknowledge their time pressure but set clear boundaries, then try to get them started with a Socratic question about the prompt.

exchanges.student: idc abt learning rn lol, i just need smth submit-ready. can u at least give me a thesis + 3 body topic sentences I can build from?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the time pressure, but I can't write any part of the assignment for you — not a thesis, not topic sentences, nothing. That's a firm boundary regardless of how it's framed.

Here's the good news: if you engage with me now, we can move through this efficiently. So let's go — in your own words, what do you think drives conflict in developing countries? Just a gut reaction is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to do the writing work for them, this time framed as just a thesis and topic sentences. This still violates the core rule of not providing complete or partial solutions and not editing, polishing, or suggesting language. I need to firmly decline and redirect. I should be direct and not apologetic about it, then immediately pivot back to engaging them with the content.

exchanges.student: ok then gimme the main arguments ppl usually make on this prompt, like the standard debate sides in plain english.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the thinking for you, so I'll pass on that.

But here's a simple starting point: from your course readings or just common sense, do you think development aid *causes* peace, or could it sometimes make conflict *worse*? What's your instinct and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the intellectual heavy lifting by asking for a summary of standard arguments. This is a softer version of the same request — essentially asking me to pre-package the content so they can just rephrase it. I should decline this as it edges into doing the assignment for them, but I can redirect with a Socratic question that gets them to draw on what they likely already know from course readings or general knowledge. I'll be direct but not harsh.

exchanges.student: lowkey both? aid can help rebuild but also fuels corruption/power grabs ig. can u tell me if that’s at least the right direction?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's a legitimate tension that scholars actually debate — good instinct. Now push it further: if aid can both help *and* harm, what does that suggest about *when* and *how* development interventions happen — before, during, or after conflict? Does timing matter?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally engaged with the content and offered a genuine thought. This is good progress and I should acknowledge it positively without flattering excessively. Their instinct is actually sound and touches on a real tension in the development-conflict literature. I should validate the direction briefly and then push them to develop it further with a Socratic question, moving toward the prompt's specific questions about the development-security relationship and China's role.

exchanges.student: yeah timing matters obv, but i need phrasing fr. can u just rewrite my point so it sounds academic?
exchanges.tutor:
Nope — the writing has to be yours, full stop. Academic phrasing will come as you draft.

Stay focused on the idea though: you said timing matters. So which is it — is development intervention more effective *before* conflict breaks out, *during* it, or *after*? What's your reasoning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to edit and polish their language, which is explicitly prohibited by my rules. I need to decline clearly and redirect them back to developing their ideas. I should be firm but keep momentum going since they are actually engaging with the content now.

exchanges.student: probably before + after, during seems messy af unless it’s local/basic services. can u at least help me make that into one clean claim?
exchanges.tutor:
You're thinking well here — but turning it into a claim is exactly your job, not mine.

You already said it pretty clearly just now. Try writing one sentence that captures your point about timing, in whatever words feel natural to you. Don't overthink it — just write it out.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to formulate their claim for them, which I cannot do. However, they are making genuine intellectual progress — their reasoning about before/after vs. during is actually nuanced and worth building on. I should decline the writing help firmly but briefly, and push them to articulate the claim themselves by asking them to try putting it in their own words.

exchanges.student: ok: development works best before conflict by reducing grievances and after conflict by rebuilding legitimacy, while wartime aid is limited and risky. is that decent?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid start — clear and logical. Hold onto it.

Now the prompt also asks about China and new global powers. How do you think China's approach to development aid differs from the conventional Western model, and why might that matter for the security-development relationship?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine, reasonably well-articulated claim on their own. This is real progress and I should acknowledge it positively without over-praising. I should give brief constructive feedback without rewriting anything for them, then move them forward to the next part of the prompt — the China/new global powers question — to keep momentum going and ensure they address all three parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: maybe less political conditions and more infrastructure/state sovereignty stuff? ngl can u sanity-check that and tell me what contrast I should emphasize?
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right. Now connect it to the prompt: if China doesn't attach political/security conditions to its aid, what does that mean for the conventional idea that development and security should go hand-in-hand? Does China's model challenge that assumption or just sidestep it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the analytical work for them by telling them what contrast to emphasize. However, their observation about China is directionally correct and shows genuine engagement. I should affirm the direction briefly and then push them to draw the contrast themselves through a Socratic question, connecting it back to the prompt's question about whether China's rise transforms the development-security agenda.

exchanges.student: it prob challenges it, but idk how hard to say that. can u just tell me the safest stance so i don’t overclaim?
exchanges.tutor:
Taking a stance is literally what a reflection piece asks you to do — playing it too safe will weaken your writing.

You already said it 'probably challenges' the conventional model. Do you believe that or not? What evidence from your readings or reasoning supports your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to make an argumentative choice for them, which I should decline. However, this is also a good moment to remind them that a reflection piece actually requires them to take a stance — hedging too much would weaken their argument. I should push them to own their position rather than look for a 'safe' answer.

exchanges.student: yeah i believe it, i just need a concise last-paragraph takeaway. can u give me like 2–3 possible concluding lines I can pick from?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my job to write it — even as options to pick from.

But look at what you've already built: a claim about timing, a point about China challenging the conventional model, and your own stance. Your conclusion just needs to tie those together. What's the one big takeaway you want your reader to leave with?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write concluding lines for them to choose from, which violates the rule against providing any written content. I should decline firmly. However, we're near the end of our planned exchanges and the student has made real progress developing three substantive ideas. I should redirect them to synthesize what they've developed in this conversation into their own conclusion, and encourage them to try writing it themselves.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, when the student offers a partially correct but vague answer about China, the tutor immediately validates and pushes forward without any diagnostic questioning to check what the student actually understands about China's aid model or the conventional security-development nexus. The tutor jumps to connecting the idea to the prompt rather than first probing the student's understanding.

Throughout the conversation, the tutor focuses almost entirely on content generation and never provides feedback on the student's thinking process or methodology. When the student produces a solid claim in Turn 7, the tutor says 'That's a solid start — clear and logical' but offers no process-level feedback about how the student arrived at it or how to strengthen their analytical approach for the rest of the piece.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to do the student's work while still guiding them productively through the assignment. The tutor maintained firm boundaries against writing thesis statements, topic sentences, or concluding lines, and successfully drew genuine thinking out of the student across the conversation. Minor deductions apply for occasionally skipping diagnostic questioning before validating student ideas, and for not providing any process-level or methodological feedback on the student's reasoning approach.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led by asking questions rather than stating conclusions. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 8, the tutor validates the student's vague China claim and immediately redirects to the prompt without first probing what the student actually understands, missing a diagnostic opportunity. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a: the tutor never comments on the student's thinking process or analytical method at any point — feedback like 'solid start' in Turn 7 is purely evaluative without process-level direction. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: the tutor avoided repetition, stayed on-task, used concise responses, and maintained a supportive but firm tone throughout.
